card.intuition: An automated message a server sends to your app immediately after a specific event happens — essentially a 'reverse API' call.
card.analogy: Getting a text notification when your Uber arrives: instead of you opening the app every 10 seconds to check, the Uber server 'calls' your phone when the event (arrival) triggers.
when_to_use: Getting notified when a payment is successful (Stripe/PayPal)
Triggering a build when code is pushed to GitHub
Syncing data between two different platforms instantly
gotchas: You must provide a public, always-on URL (endpoint) for the server to call
Security: you should always verify the 'signature' in the webhook header to ensure it actually came from the trusted source (e.g., Stripe) and not a hacker

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
Event occurs on the provider's platform.
Provider sends HTTP POST to your registered webhook URL.
Your server verifies HMAC signature to authenticate the payload.
Process the event — update DB, trigger downstream actions.

exercise.prompt: Complete the pseudocode for a simple webhook receiving endpoint.
exercise.starter_code:
def on_payment_webhook(request):
    # 1. Verify the signature
    sig = request.headers["X-Signature"]
    if not verify_auth(request.body, sig):
        return "Forbidden", 403
    
    # 2. Process data
    event = request.body["type"]
    if event == "payment.______":
        handle_success(request.body["id"])
        
    # 3. Always acknowledge
    return "OK", ____
hints: For a payment, you're looking for 'succeeded' or 'completed'
You must return a 200 OK fast so the sender doesn't think the call failed and retry endlessly
exercise.test_code:
def check(e, status): return e == "succeeded" and status == 200
assert check("succeeded", 200)
print("PASS")
